examples.title: Checking Command Types Script
examples.description: Script demonstrating use of type to check command types before execution
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Script to demonstrate type command usage

check_command() {
 local cmd=$1
 local cmd_type=$(type -t "$cmd" 2>/dev/null)
 
 if [ -z "$cmd_type" ]; then
 echo "$cmd: command not found"
 return 1
 fi
 
 case "$cmd_type" in
 alias)
 echo "$cmd is an alias"
 type -a "$cmd"
 ;;
 keyword)
 echo "$cmd is a shell keyword"
 ;;
 function)
 echo "$cmd is a shell function"
 type -a "$cmd"
 ;;
 builtin)
 echo "$cmd is a shell builtin"
 ;;
 file)
 echo "$cmd is an executable file"
 type -p "$cmd"
 ;;
 esac
}

# Test with various commands
check_command "ls"
check_command "cd"
check_command "if"
check_command "myfunction"
examples.explanation: This script uses `type -t` to get single-word type and `type -a` to show all occurrences. It demonstrates practical use of type for command validation in shell scripts.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) - depends on PATH size for file searches

examples.title: Create Command Override Test
examples.description: Test to demonstrate command resolution order with aliases, functions, and builtins
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Demonstrate command resolution order

# Step 1: Create an alias with same name as a file
alias testcmd='echo "I am an alias"'

# Step 2: Create a function with same name
testcmd() {
 echo "I am a function"
}

# Step 3: Test type command to see resolution
echo "=== Testing type command resolution ==="
echo "type -t testcmd: $(type -t testcmd)"
echo "type -a testcmd:"
type -a testcmd

# Step 4: Remove alias to see function
alias -d testcmd
echo "After removing alias, type -t: $(type -t testcmd)"

# Step 5: Unset function to see builtin or file
unset -f testcmd
echo "After removing function, type -t: $(type -t testcmd)"
examples.explanation: This script demonstrates how `type` shows the priority order: aliases override functions, which override builtins, which override files. Shows practical understanding of shell resolution.

examples.title: Type Command Option Logic
examples.description: Pseudocode showing how type command processes different options
examples.code:
FUNCTION type_command(command, options):
 IF option == "-t":
 # Return single word type
 result = get_command_type(command)
 PRINT result[0] # alias/keyword/function/builtin/file
 
 ELSE IF option == "-a":
 # Return all matches
 IF is_alias(command):
 PRINT "command is aliased to..."
 IF is_keyword(command):
 PRINT "command is a shell keyword"
 IF is_function(command):
 PRINT "command is a function"
 IF is_builtin(command):
 PRINT "command is a shell builtin"
 IF is_file_in_path(command):
 PRINT "command is... (path)"
 
 ELSE IF option == "-p":
 # Return only file path
 IF is_file_in_path(command):
 PRINT get_path(command)
 ELSE:
 PRINT nothing
 
 ELSE IF option == "-P":
 # Force PATH search
 path = search_path(command)
 PRINT path
 
 ELSE IF option == "-f":
 # Suppress function lookup
 type = get_type_without_function(command)
 PRINT type

FUNCTION get_command_type(command):
 IF is_alias(command): RETURN ["alias"]
 ELSE IF is_keyword(command): RETURN ["keyword"]
 ELSE IF is_function(command): RETURN ["function"]
 ELSE IF is_builtin(command): RETURN ["builtin"]
 ELSE IF is_file_in_path(command): RETURN ["file"]
 ELSE RETURN ["not found"]
examples.explanation: Pseudocode showing the logic flow of type command with different options. Demonstrates understanding of how type processes each option differently.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) - depends on PATH and number of definitions
